title: Chip-carved pine breadboard
subtitle: A pine serving board with a chip-carved border design
excerpt: A pine breadboard with a chip-carved geometric border. Pine is softer than hardwoods but carves cleanly with the grain and takes a chip-cut border well on the flat face. The board is finished with board butter.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the chip-carving method on flat board stock; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the breadboard household-utensil form.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable moisture content of 8 to 12 percent for indoor use.
Three or more cuts converging to lift a small triangular chip from the surface. The chip-carving fundamental.
A short vertical cut into the wood that stops the next push or pull cut from tearing the grain past the intended end-point.
The slot a saw cut leaves; in carving, the cut a knife makes that is open enough to be visible. The V-channels in chip carving are visible kerfs.

content.text: Chip carving on a flat board is the starting point for decorative wood carving: the tools are few, the design is geometric, and the
seasoned

pine surface shows the work clearly. A pine breadboard serves double duty as a cutting and serving board; the chip-carved border marks it as made rather than bought. Pine is softer than beech or sycamore but still takes a clean chip cut when the knife is sharp and the cuts go with the grain.
Wood

Planed pine board 200 mm wide, 250 mm long, and 18 mm thick is the recommended starting material. Straight-grained, knot-free sections chip cleanly; a knot in the border area will cause a chip cut to run unpredictably. Source from a timber merchant as a planed-all-round (PAR) board. Spruce is a close alternative if pine is unavailable. For a harder board that will survive heavier chopping use, substitute beech at the same dimensions.
Tools
Sloyd knife: for all chip cutting and for rounding the board corners.
Marking knife (optional): for scoring the design lines before chip cutting. A marking knife scores more precisely than a pencil line.
Try square, 200 mm: for laying out the border lines square to the edges.
Marking gauge: for scribing the border line parallel to the board edge.
Sandpaper, 320 grit: for the board face and edges before carving and finishing.
Leather strop: strop every fifteen minutes.
Board butter: the food-safe wax-and-oil finish for the serving surface.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Preparing the board
Sand both faces with 320-grit to remove any mill marks. Round the four corners gently with the sloyd knife: a 10 mm radius gives the board a softer profile and prevents corner splitting.
Laying out the design

Set the marking gauge to 15 mm and scribe a line parallel to all four edges. This line is the inner boundary of the chip-carved border. Within the 15 mm band, mark a regular pattern of equilateral triangles with the pencil or marking knife. Each triangle is 12 mm on a side, pointing alternately toward the board centre and toward the board edge. The result is a running border of star-point triangles: the design that appears in Cassell's as the simplest chip-carving repeat.
Chip cutting the border
Each triangle in the border is cut with three
chip cuts
. Set a
stop cut

first along each side of the triangle: a shallow vertical cut 1.5 mm deep directly on the pencil line. Then make the angled cuts from each side toward the centre, the blade angled at 45 degrees to the surface. The chip should release cleanly from all three corners simultaneously. If it does not release, re-set the stop cuts deeper and try again.
Work around the border in sections: do all the stop cuts for one side, then return and take the chip cuts. This rhythm keeps the stop cuts fresh in the grain when the angle cuts arrive. The finished
kerf
in each triangle should show clean sloping faces from the walls to the flat bottom.
Finishing

Brush away the chips with a soft brush. Apply board butter with a soft cloth over the whole board including the border. Let stand twenty minutes, then buff off. The wax-and-oil paste fills the open chip-carving triangles slightly, which mutes them to a softer register; a thinner coat over the carved areas keeps the definition crisper.
Variations
Notch-and-triangle border: combine the triangle chips with simple notch cuts for a more complex geometric pattern.
Round board: cut a 200 mm diameter circle from a pine slab and carve the border around the circumference.
Handle: add a 50 mm handle cutout at one end using a coping saw before carving the border.
Care
Hand-wash only; never dishwasher and never long soak. Dry promptly. Re-apply board butter monthly or when the board looks dry.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: A chip tore rather than lifting cleanly
attrs.cause: Grain ran across the triangle rather than parallel to one side
attrs.fix: In pine the grain direction changes at knots and figure lines. Chips near a grain interruption need lighter stop cuts and lower-angle chip cuts. Try cutting from the other direction across the same chip.

attrs.symptom: The border pattern has uneven gaps between triangles
attrs.cause: Triangles not laid out consistently from a baseline
attrs.fix: Use the try square and a stepped grid from one corner. Mark all triangles before cutting any; adjustments are easier at the pencil stage.

attrs.symptom: The chip faces have rough ridges rather than flat slopes
attrs.cause: Knife dull or cut direction wrong
attrs.fix: Strop before every six to eight chips in pine. The soft grain loads the edge quickly. The angle cut must travel with the grain direction, not against it; a torn face means the cut went uphill.
